title: Chapter 10: Exploring the Glitch
content: /* Speed: Breather | D5+D12 */
The security camera in the pharmacy window showed a pixel smear where Wren stood — a vertical stripe of corrupted data, like a barcode made of static. She lifted her hand. The smear stretched upward.
"Okay," she whispered. "Camera: broken."

She pulled out the notebook — a leather journal she'd taken from a stationery store three days ago, unashamed. Her handwriting was small and tight, the architecture of someone trained to draw, not write. She'd started a grid: columns for what worked, what didn't, what she wasn't sure about.
CAMERAS — she wrote — Show pixel corruption. Can't be captured. Confirmed x4 locations.
The list had been growing all morning.

She'd started at her building's front entrance. The lobby camera showed a faint blur when she walked through, like heat haze. The elevator camera: same. The corner store around the block had two cameras — one showed nothing at all, the other the same barcode smear. Four data points, consistent.

MOTION SENSORS: She'd walked through three automatic doors. Two opened normally, as if she were there. One didn't. The difference? The two that opened were older — infrared-based. The one that didn't was newer, ultrasonic.
She wrote: Older motion sensors pick me up. Newer ones don't. Something about the frequency?

VOICE ASSISTANTS: She'd stood in an electronics store for twenty minutes, saying "Hey Alexa" and "Hey Google" at varying volumes. Nothing. The smart speakers sat inert, their little LED rings dark. She wasn't even a ghost to them — she was simply not a variable.

MIRRORS: This was the strangest one. She'd stood in front of the mirrored wall in a clothing store for ten minutes. Nothing. No reflection. Not even a shimmer. The mirror showed exactly what was behind her — racks of dresses, a sale sign, the fluorescents — as though she were a hole in the world cut with an X-Acto knife.

She looked at her hands. They were there. She felt them — the callus on her right middle finger from years of pencil work, the scar on her left thumb from a kitchen accident when she was fifteen, the chipped navy polish on three nails. She could see them when she looked down. But the mirror saw nothing.
Her stomach growled. She ignored it and wrote: Self-perception intact. External perception: zero. Disconnect between internal and external registration.

The journal was filling up. Page after page of neat, methodical notes. It made her feel calmer — the act of observation, of documentation. If she could map the edges of this thing, she could find the seams. Everything had seams.

She moved to a bench in a small park — the kind of pocket park sandwiched between buildings, two trees and a fountain that didn't work. An older man sat on the other end, feeding pigeons from a paper bag. He didn't look up when she sat down.
A kid ran past, chasing a soccer ball. The ball rolled under the bench. The kid dropped to his knees, reached underneath, and his hand brushed Wren's ankle.
He froze. Looked directly at the space where she sat. His brow furrowed.
"There's something here," he said.
Wren held her breath.
The kid's mother called from across the park. "Jamie! Come on!"
He looked back at his mother, looked at the space under the bench one more time, then grabbed his ball and ran.
Wren exhaled. Another note: CHILDREN — closer to detecting? Possible. Need more data.
She turned the page and started a new section: HYPOTHESES.

1. The glitch is electromagnetic — some field I'm generating that disrupts digital recording.
2. The glitch is perceptual — human brains are being filtered. The kid nearly broke through.
3. The glitch is both.
She stared at the third option. Circled it twice.

The fountain gurgled — someone must have turned it on remotely. The water caught the light in a way that made her fingers itch for a pencil. She sketched it quickly in the margin: the broken fountain, the pigeon man, the vertical smear of a barcode where her shadow should have been.
Then she noticed the streetlight.

It was a modern LED pole — one of the smart ones the city had installed last year, with sensors that adjusted brightness based on pedestrian traffic. It was midday, so the light was off. But as Wren stared at it, something tugged at the back of her skull. A pressure, like the beginning of a headache but directional. Pushing outward from behind her temples toward the lamp.
She stared harder. The pressure built.
The light flickered.
Just once — a brief, stuttering pulse of white — and then dark again.
Wren's hand shot out and grabbed the bench. Her pulse spiked. She hadn't touched anything. She hadn't thrown anything. She'd just — looked. Pushed.
She did it again. Stared at the light. Pushed with whatever that thing was behind her forehead.
Flicker. Fainter this time. But real.
"Holy shit," she breathed.
The pigeon man glanced at the light, then went back to his bag. No reaction. Probably thought it was a glitch in the grid. He wasn't wrong.
She pushed again. This time, the light stayed on for two full seconds before dying. A woman walking past looked up, annoyed, then continued.
Wren opened her notebook with trembling hands.
NEW CATEGORY: Influence on electronic systems.
She stood and walked toward the lamp. Stood directly under it. Pushed.
The light came on — full brightness — and then the fixture started buzzing. Not the quiet hum of operation but an angry, electrical whine. She backed off. The light died. The buzzing stopped.
Too much. She'd pushed too hard.
She walked to a different pole, fifty feet away. This time, she tried something gentler — not a push but a nudge. A whisper instead of a shout.
The light flickered once. Perfectly controlled. On, off. Like she'd flipped a switch.
Satisfaction bloomed in her chest — small, cautious, but real. For the first time since her phone had stopped recognizing her face, she felt something other than helpless.
She spent the next hour walking the neighborhood, testing. Results were inconsistent but not random. She could influence:

- LED lights (moderate success, 60%)
- Older CRT monitors in a thrift store (high success, the screen scrambled like a magnet had been dragged across it)
- A digital parking meter (briefly displayed garbage characters, then reset)
- A ATM screen (flickered, then went to a diagnostic menu she couldn't navigate — she backed off quickly)

She could NOT influence:
- Mechanical objects — doorknobs, faucets, manual locks
- Battery-powered devices smaller than a phone
- Her own phone, maddeningly
But the phone was separate. The phone was the thing that had broken first. The phone was patient zero.
She sat on a low wall outside a laundromat, the warm hum of dryers bleeding through the glass, and wrote it all down. Her handwriting had gotten messier — excitement overriding precision.
The unknown number texted again.
*You're reaching. Good. Stage 4 is near. Be careful with the push — it takes more than you think.*
She read it three times. Stage 4. Near. More than you think.
What did it take? She felt fine. A little tired, maybe. A headache building at the base of her skull. The same headache she got when she drew for too long without eating.
She touched her temple. Warm. The pressure was still there, faint but present, like a muscle she'd flexed for the first time.
She pushed again, idly, at the laundromat's OPEN sign in the window.
It flickered. The 'O' went dark for a second, then returned.
A kid inside looked up at the sign, frowned, and went back to his phone.
Wren let the pressure go. The headache throbbed once, twice, then faded to a dull hum.
She had rules now. She had limits. She had the first fragile handle on something that might, maybe, be control.
It wasn't much. But it was more than she'd had yesterday.

She closed the notebook and slid it into her bag. Down the block, a street musician was playing violin — something classical and sad, the notes rising and falling like breathing. The accordion doors of a restaurant opened, releasing a cloud of garlic and rosemary. A dog barked. A taxi honked.
The world was loud and present and had no idea she was in it.
But she could flicker its lights.
That was something.
That was definitely something.
